# Transcrição: 01-WEBINAR

- **Fonte:** 01-WEBINAR
- **Duração:** 30:40
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:00]

Hey, everyone. My name is Matthew Dix, and I'm going to be talking today about storytelling for leaders. For those people who are in charge of teams and in charge of companies, how can you deploy storytelling into the work that you do to help improve the culture and the climate and the work that people do and help you impact those people in a more productive way? I could speak about this topic for a very long period of time. Today, I'm going to give you three excellent strategies that you can begin using as a leader today in order to improve the company, the group, the team, the organization that you work in simply by deploying storytelling in effective ways. I'm going to do this by telling you some stories. I'm going to give you some examples of how these three strategies have been used in my life, either by leaders who I have interacted with or people I work with on a regular basis as a consultant and hopefully in doing so, you can glean some of that wisdom, learn some of those strategies and begin using these strategies tomorrow when you return to work. All right. So strategy number one, making moments in the workplace. I think one of the things that leaders fail to do repeatedly and constantly is they fail to take advantage of real moments in the workplace. Essentially, what they do is they take the simplest and easiest route to anything that they do rather than finding a way to really make a moment out of it. We try to make moments out of things because moments become stories. They become stories that we can tell or they are stories that employees will eventually tell about themselves, their workplace and the work they do. Let me give you an example. About 10 years, I'm sitting in the auditorium in the school where I teach. It's a Friday assembly. We have one every Friday. It's called town meeting. And my principal, Dr. Plato Caraphalus, is at the front of the auditorium and he is leading this assembly and he makes an announcement. He says, all right, for the next segment, I need the following teachers to come and stand on the stage in a row side by side. And my name is called. I have no idea why I'm being called down, which is surprising to me because I often have a role in this town meeting. I'm one who prepares and oftentimes I co-host it and when my principal is around, I am the host of town meetings. So I'm usually aware of everything that's going to be happening, but now I've been called down with 10 of my fellow teachers. So we take the stage and we sort of stand in a row in front of 500 kids and a couple hundred parents and about 100 staff members. And I'm standing there thinking, why are we here? And then Dr. Caraphalus tells us that over the last month, he has assembled a team of students and they have created a rubric, a criteria, and they have taken this criteria and they have snuck into each one of our classrooms, every single teacher in the building for one specific purpose, to check out the chair behind the teacher's desk because he knew that there were some terrible teacher chairs in our school. A lot of the teachers in our school were suffering from really terrible chairs and he decided to do

## [03:00 - 06:01]

something about it. So he gathered some students together. He formed a committee. He created a rubric, a criteria. They had clipboards and pens and they secretly crept around the building and whenever a teacher left their classroom for some reason, the committee snuck in, they examined the chair, they completed the rubric, and then they left. And then eventually they met back with the principal and they had a meeting and they identified the 10 teachers who had the 10 worst chairs in the school and the 10 teachers standing on the stage that day. Those were the 10. The 10 of us had the worst chairs of anyone in the school and I knew it because I had a terrible chair. And so then he announced, he said, you 10 have been chosen to receive brand new chairs, not by me, not by any of your colleagues, but by the students. They have identified you as the ones most in need of better chairs. So congratulations, you'll be receiving a new chair. And at that moment, the curtain behind us flew open and behind each one of us perfectly aligned to where we were standing was a brand new chair. And Dr. Caraphaelus Plato, my friend, said, go ahead and take a seat in your brand new chair. In each one of us turned around, we saw a chair, we sat down in our new chair and all of the kids in the audience went crazy and the adults clapped and the staff members clapped and probably a few of them were jealous thinking we have terrible chairs too, but we were the 10 that were chosen that day. That was a moment that I remember from more than a decade ago, the day I got a new chair. What I think happens in most schools, most offices, most workplaces is when someone needs a new chair, either they're identified as having a bad chair or they complain about their chair often enough to eventually get a new chair. The boss comes around and says, hey, I got you a new chair and they roll it into the cubicle, they roll it into the office or they say, go pick it up at the loading dock and then you get your new chair. And that's how it works almost always. Plato Caraphaelus understood that making moments creates culture. Plato Caraphaelus understood that when you make a moment out of something as simple as getting 10 teachers, 10 new chairs, when you make it something like that in the middle of an assembly as a real moment of surprise and suspense and fun, people remember it. People talk about it, people tell stories about it. I just told you a story about it. No, also he involved a whole bunch of people in that process. He involved students, about a dozen students were in a committee that got to sneak around the building. They got to creep into teachers classrooms, they had lookouts. Like it was fun for the kids. The kids get to create a rubric meet with the principal. It's a story for them too. It's probably a story they remember to this day. Those kids are now graduated from college and they're adults and they probably remember being assigned to a committee and sneaking around the school and determining which teachers got new chairs that year. Those kids now have a story to tell. I have a story to tell. Some of my colleagues have a story to tell through the simplicity of giving me a new chair. He made a moment out of it and it created

## [06:01 - 09:05]

a story. What you want to do is the same thing. As a leader, you want to be seeking these moments that are small and seemingly insignificant to non storytellers, to leaders who don't understand how to build culture. And you want to take those moments that are otherwise insignificant to most people and make them something really significant. Make them something people will always remember. We build culture through storytelling by speaking about the past, by conveying the things that have happened before us and bringing them into the present to let us know that we're part of a bigger picture. Now let me give you an example of an opportunity that was missed also in my school. About five years ago, I was playing mat ball with a bunch of students during field day. Mat ball is essentially kick ball inside the gym. Kids run around and it's called mat ball because instead of a base, there's a mat lying on the ground and multiple kids can stand on the mat. It's kind of a fun game. It's actually perfect for indoors when you have to play kick ball indoors. But there was a rule in mat ball because it's played inside the gym. If anyone ever kicks the ball and it goes through the basketball hoop on the other side of the gym, that's 100 runs. And in the 20 years that mat ball had been played in our school, no one had ever even come close to kicking a ball through that hoop. And then one day, about five years ago, I was pitching the ball to a student and they kicked it and it went through the hoop. And I couldn't believe it. It was 100 runs. And there was another teacher in the gym at the time, another physical education teacher. And I turned to that teacher and I said, we got to pick that kid up. And he said, what? And I said, we got to pick him up. We got to put him on our shoulders. We got to run him around the gym. This is a moment. And the physical education teacher didn't quite get it right away. Eventually he did. And we picked the kid up and we ran him around the, ran him around the gym and the kids followed. It was a real moment. It was wonderful. And then at the end of field day, I went to our physical education teacher and I said, listen, you need to make a plaque. You need to make a hundred run plaque and put this kid's name on it and hang it in the gym so that this moment is memorialized so that kids can see that kid's name, that kid can see their name and that moving forward, kids now have something to shoot for. Someone actually did it. Someone did the impossible. They kicked the ball and it went through the basketball hoop for 100 runs. Maybe it will happen again. That plaque does not exist today. That teacher allowed that moment to go by. And I think that's a disaster. I think whenever we miss the opportunity to make something meaningful from a moment, small, like passing out chairs to teachers or big, like doing something that has never been done before, when we miss those moments, we miss the opportunity to tell stories and to build culture and to make our people feel like they're part of something larger and more important and more ongoing. I think that's what we have to do as leaders. So over the course of the next week,

## [09:05 - 12:10]

here's what you do. Think about all the fairly mundane tasks that you have to accomplish. Think about all of the fairly ordinary tasks that your people accomplish. Think about moments that you might be able to make into something more than mundane and ordinary. Try to find a moment or an opportunity to really make something special for someone. Think about things that you do and that your people do that are important but unheralded. How can you make them into something wherein the person you are honoring or the moment you are celebrating becomes the story they tell at the dinner table that night? Because when I went home that night, the story I told my family was how I got a new chair and the means by which I was given it. So inventory your week, the coming week, look at all the things that are happening. Look at all the things that are going on and ask yourself which one of these can be made into something special, something really meaningful, something that people will never forget. That's strategy one. Strategy number two. Strategy number two. Honor people and your organization through storytelling. This is one of the easiest things you can do and it is tragically done so infrequently that it breaks my heart. Let me tell you another story about Dr. Plato Caraphaelus. The principal of my school, by the way, for 25 years. 25 years as the principal of any school is unheard of. It is astounding and ridiculous and you only become the principal of a school for 25 years if you know what you're doing, which Plato knew every day. So there was a day about 15 years ago when our school district announced that each school was going to be receiving a gazebo for the background of their school, a place where people can gather and if you need to have a ceremony of some kind, this gazebo will be useful. And so when Plato heard that, he immediately realized he had an opportunity, a storytelling opportunity. This is not just going to be a gazebo. He was going to make sure that when the gazebo arrived that it was going to be dedicated to someone, that someone's name was going to be on that gazebo. Otherwise, it was a missed opportunity, which is what happens all the time in business today. And so once again, Plato assembled a committee. This was a committee of teachers and the debate began. Who should we dedicate the gazebo to? Ultimately, we decided to dedicate the gazebo to Jerry Nardi. Jerry Nardi was a former custodian at our school who had passed away recently. He had retired and then tragically less than a year after his retirement, he passed away. He was the custodian at our school for more than 20 years. And the teachers and Plato decided he was the person we were going to honor. And one of the ways we would honor Jerry Nardi is to bring back his widow and his children and let them know that we were naming a gazebo after him. And so Plato arranged a very significant

## [12:10 - 15:12]

ceremony on the day we opened that gazebo. There was a red ribbon that got cut. All of the men in the building, there were about eight of them at the time, sang an acapella song. We all wore the same outfits. I have a photo of it. It's hilarious. Speeches were delivered talking about how Jerry Nardi had made an impact on everyone's lives over the years. It was a really meaningful and important ceremony. It was meaningful and important to anyone who knew Jerry at the time. And it was meaningful and important to his widow and to his children. I barely knew Jerry Nardi. I almost didn't know him at all. In fact, without that ceremony and without that gazebo, I would be unable to tell you what his name is today. But because of that ceremony and because there's a gazebo still behind my school today with a gold plaque on it that lists Jerry's name. I know Jerry Nardi. I remember stories about Jerry Nardi and I know who took care of our school for more than two decades. It's because rather than simply planting a gazebo behind the school, which is what happened at almost every school in our district, Plato understood, here's an opportunity to do something special. I was recently touring the FBI. I work with their hostage negotiation unit. And as I was touring their facilities at Quantico, I saw how they embraced storytelling in a similar way. Conference rooms are not labeled as conference room A, B, and C. Conference rooms at Quantico are named after former FBI agents who have done heroic things. The walls at Quantico are filled with plaques that feature moments in the history of the FBI, recent history, in which agents did extraordinary things. They include newspaper clippings and photos. And for whenever they can sort of FBI documentation of the event. And as you walk through the building, you cannot help but see all of these plaques on all of these walls. Also, there's an FBI training program, which is essentially almost impossible to get through alive. They graduate almost no one through this process to sort of become the highest trained FBI agents in the business. These are people who were Navy SEALs and decided that wasn't hard enough. We're going to go to the FBI and try to become the highest of the high and Navy SEALs wash out of this program. But those that do not wash out, they get their name on a plaque, a really significant plaque in a significant space, a space of honor at the FBI, everywhere throughout the FBI. There is the names of people who have done extraordinary things. But you don't need to be working for the FBI in order to do this. You can also be the custodian at an elementary school. Think about where you work. Think about the building that you occupy. Are there offices and conference rooms that are labeled ABC or 123

## [15:12 - 18:14]

that could instead be named after people who have done significant things for your company? Is that something that you could possibly do? If you work in a building that is four stories tall and it has an elevator, can you name that elevator after someone? Can you name the elevator after the person in your company who began in the mailroom and managed to climb up to an executive position? The person who climbed that ladder? Name that elevator after that person and make it a moment. Make it a moment where that person is going to be honored or that person's family is going to be honored on their behalf. Include red ribbons and music and speeches. Carve out 30 minutes of the work day to convert what would otherwise be an ordinary conference room into a place where people walk by that plaque, a story of that person, the memory of that person lands in their mind. I see that gazebo every single day. And every single day when I look at that gazebo, I think of Jerry Nardi. That is how we create culture. That is how we make people feel valued in the organization. They know that we will continue to tell their stories long after they have left. And when you can feel that way in an organization, you feel part of something important and you believe in it. My classroom is filled with artifacts given to me by students or artifacts that cause me to remember students. Strange objects are on shelves and hanging on the walls. And my students every year come in and they say, why is there a Barbie doll up on that bookshelf? Why is there an unopened present over here on your desk? What is this trophy sitting here on this table? Objects all over my classroom and every one of them has a story attached to it. And eventually I tell the kids the story of all the objects, which is to say the story of the children whose memories are attached to those objects. And the students invariably say, are you going to tell stories about me someday? What stories are you going to tell the future kids? They want to know that I am going to speak of them in the same way I speak of former students once they have gone. Adults are the same way. Adults want to occupy spaces and organizations and groups. They want to know that the place where they are spending their time is a place that will remember them when they have gone. They want to know that whether they take a new job or they retire or they switch departments, that when they leave, when they take that brown box of stuff off their desk and they shuffle off to the next mortal coil, they want to know that they will have left a mark and impact. And we do that through storytelling. So this week, inventory, your facility, your office, your building, your workspace, are there rooms that don't have names that could be named after people?

## [18:14 - 21:15]

Are there elevators and hallways and restrooms that could be named after someone? Is that a possibility? Do you have parking spots outside in the parking lot that could be named in honor of people? Or could you take a parking spot and identify it as an employee of the month parking spot? And so that every month, you have a ceremony where that employee of the month is identified and the parking spot is bestowed upon them. Can you do things like that? Can you find ways to tell stories about the people who work in your organization so that the people working alongside them understand that they too will be remembered? That if they do the same good work that the person being honored for does, that if they work as hard as the person being honored, if they work as cleverly and intelligently and adeptly as the person who's being honored, they too will be remembered. People want to be remembered. They don't want to think that the time they're spending in this place is meaningless. We let them know that it's not meaningless by telling stories, and we let them know that those stories are going to be told by leaving markers of those people in those places. So inventory your workplace this week. As a leader, look around. Find the things that you can place a name on, the name of someone who you think should be remembered, and begin doing it. That's strategy number two. Strategy number three is probably the easiest of all of them, which is tell stories so that your people know who they are. And this is a very simple one. Think back on your school inquiry. If you're like me, you had two types of teachers. You had the majority of your teachers who operated like black boxes, meaning you didn't know if they were married. You didn't know the names of their kids. You didn't know what their home life was like. You didn't know if they had pets. They were essentially teachers and nothing more. Most of my teachers were that way growing up. Most of my teachers were teachers and nothing more. And that's how it is in a lot of schools. That's why when a student sees their teacher in the grocery store, they can't believe it. Because in their mind, their teacher is only a teacher because that's all the teacher ever talks about. Those are black box teachers. There are leaders who are the same, black box leaders, people who go into the workplace and talk about work all day, but never really speak about who they are in terms of their family, their likes, their dislikes, their hobbies, the sports they play, the books they read, the movies they love, those kinds of things. Those are black box leaders. Those are not people we believe in. Those are monoliths. Those are round white and flavorless human beings who are seeking to be innocuous. And we do not believe in people who

## [21:15 - 24:17]

are innocuous. We do not get inspired by round white and flavorless. We're inspired by human beings. And the more human you can be as a leader, the better off you're going to be. We can do this in a bunch of ways and I have many ways to teach leaders, but the one I'm going to talk about today is a simple one, which is essentially bringing stories into your workplace as often as possible. Bringing stories and understanding that sharing of yourself is just as important as celebrating the accomplishments of others. I'm going to say that again because most leaders tend not to believe this, but sharing of yourself, presenting yourself as an authentic, genuine human being, is just as important as sharing the accomplishments of others. As a teacher, I know this for sure. I tell more stories about my life to my students than perhaps any teacher in the history of the world. I share stories about my life with my students constantly and the results are pretty extraordinary. I am not the best teacher when it comes to lesson planning, pedagogy, and understanding of the curriculum. I am not the best teacher in any of those regards. My colleagues are far superior to me in all of those skills. My colleagues tell stories too, but I think the one thing that I do well, the one thing I do exceptionally well is I tell my kids stories relentlessly about myself, not in an effort to evangelize myself or to create a legacy for myself, but to show my kids, show my students who I am as a person and how my experiences as a human being can help them make better decisions in their lives. It also creates an enormous bond of trust between my students and I, which is why when my female students have their first period during fifth grade, which happens quite a bit, and I am in a building occupied almost exclusively by women, the number of men in my school I can count on one hand and still have fingers left and the number of women in my building, I would need dozens of hands to count them all. And yet when a female student has her first period, she often comes to me, a person who is quite frankly ill-equipped to help her. So a student will come to me and say, Mr. Dix, I just had my first period, and I will always say something to the effect of thanks for trusting me with that. I don't use the products that you require. Let me go find someone who does, which is what I say every time. And I'm not surprised they say it to me. I was surprised at first, but today I am not. I'm not surprised because I tell stories about being embarrassed and ashamed and feeling awkward all the time. So when a girl has her first period and I can only imagine what it is like, whether she's feeling embarrassed, ashamed, awkward, whatever, she knows that that guy understands the feelings that I'm now having and so I can go talk to him. That is a student-teacher relationship, but you can establish the same

## [24:17 - 27:19]

relationships as a leader. You obviously occupy a position that is different than your employees. You are one rung or more up the ladder. That doesn't mean that you can't occupy the same rung of the ladder when it comes to your humanity, which means when you come to work and someone says, hey, boss, how was your weekend? You don't say great. You say, I finished a book this weekend that I really loved. The title of it was blank and it was about blank and that tells people what you care about. When someone says, how was your weekend? You say, I sat in a folding chair at four Little League games this weekend. My son won all four of the games and played extraordinarily well, but I'm really sick of baseball right now. That says something about you as a leader. When you are willing to share of yourself, not only your accomplishments, but quite frankly, more often your struggles, your failures, your moments that are less than successful, the moments of embarrassment and awkwardness and shame, when you can share those moments with your people, they're going to begin to trust you. They're going to begin to be inspired by you. They're going to begin to see you as an authentic human being so that when you have to make a tough decision, when you have to correct something that they have done, when you have to deliver that challenging performance review, it's not coming from a monolith. It's not coming from someone who is round, white and flavorless. It's coming from a genuine human being. So spend this week asking yourself, what do my people know about me and what do they not create one of those T charts? Right? Line down the middle, line across the top, make a T. On one side, it's going to be all the things my employees know about me. And then on the right, all the things my employees don't know about me. Create that T chart. Find out what they already know, but most important, find out what they don't know. What have you never spoken about that might be worth speaking about? What have you not told them that they might want to know? What have you found too insignificant or boring or meaningless that perhaps could have some meaning to your people? Do you like to bake brownies? Do you love sushi? Do you like to watch basketball on television? Do you play volleyball in an intramural league on Thursday nights? What books do you read? How are you feeling about your children today? We know you love them, but how do you feel about them today? Do you have pets? What music do you love to listen to? What television show are you currently obsessed with? These are the things that will make you human. And each one of these can be a story. When we tell stories to the people who work for us, they get to know us, they trust us, and eventually they can be inspired by us.

## [27:20 - 30:25]

The best leaders who I know, Dr. Plato Caraphaelus, Boris Levin, a man by the name of Jalou Montessor, a McDonald's manager who inspires me to this day, even though we have not worked together for more than 25 years. All of those people were people who told me stories about them that made me adore them. The same thing with my teachers. Teachers who inspired me, Lester Moroni, Patrick Sullivan, Jackie Leblanc, Mrs. Schultz, all of those people were teachers who opened up their hearts and their lives to me through storytelling. And because of that, each one of them and many more changed my life, changed my life in deep and significant ways, in ways that other teachers were not able to do because I did not know who they were. As leaders, we have to tell stories about ourselves, whether we're comfortable about it or not. If we want people to trust us and believe in us, that is what we must do. So make that T-chart that is your job. Make that T-chart and identify all the things about you that you have yet to speak about with your employees and begin speaking about them, telling stories about them, stories that are 20 seconds long, stories that are 20 minutes long. Begin speaking to your people about who you are. Begin speaking about yourself. Begin telling them your stories and they will begin to believe in you in ways you could not begin to imagine. So I've given you three strategies and I've given you three assignments. Strategy number one, make meaning from moments. Find those things that you are doing on a daily, a weekly, a monthly basis that could be honored through a story. As simple as you're getting a new chair or as extraordinary as someone who's just done something that has never been done before. In each case, make that moment something we will remember forever. Strategy number two, honor your people through storytelling and do this by creating artifacts of those people in the spaces that you occupy. That means naming conference rooms and parking spaces and elevators after the people who have done great work. That means filling your space with artifacts that relate to human beings who once or are currently working in those spaces. Make people aware. Make people understand. Make people believe that when they are gone, when they have left this space and moved on to the next job or to the next phase of their life, that they will be remembered because you have created artifacts from which stories can be told. And strategy number three, make yourself known through storytelling. Make sure that you are sharing of yourself to the people around you. Make that T-chart. Identify the things that you have not told your people and begin telling them. Find ways to begin speaking about yourself, the things you care about, to the people who you work with so that they can begin to care about you.

## [30:25 - 30:40]

Think about you as a human being. Trust you and become inspired by the words you speak. Three storytelling strategies that leaders can begin using today in deep and meaningful ways. Good luck with all of this and have a great day.
